seoTitle: First dev after funding: 2 days/week instead of full-time | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: Post-raise startup: before hiring your first full-time developer, why a fractional Product Engineer two days a week can deliver more value in the same budget band.

hero.title: Before hiring your first full-time developer, consider two days a week
hero.lede:
You just raised. The pressure is on: ship faster, prove traction, show the team is growing. Hiring a first full-time developer feels natural — but it's often the most expensive and slowest way to get solid technical leadership.

An experienced Product Engineer two days a week can own technical direction, architecture, and delivery — in a comparable budget band, with less risk if the product pivots.

blocks.title: Why the first tech hire is risky
blocks.lede: A junior or mid full-time developer means salary + payroll burden + onboarding + management — often before the roadmap is clear enough. The founder still arbitrates product and tech alone. A bad hire is brutally expensive for a young company.

blocks.title: What two days a week give you

items.title: Same budget band, more seniority
items.content: A budget planned for a first employee can fund two days a week of a senior profile: someone who has already shaped products, teams, and delivery — not just executed tickets.

items.title: Technical direction + delivery
items.content: I help decide what to build and why, design the architecture, and ship code or guide the team. No handoff between « the strategy person » and « the delivery person ».

items.title: Flexibility if you pivot
items.content: Before locking in a full internal team, you validate pace, stack, and priorities with someone who can adapt without a layoff or emergency re-hire.

items.title: Bridge to a future team
items.content: When it's time to hire, you already have standards, a clearer backlog, and a realistic job spec — and possibly a developer I've helped onboard.

blocks.title: First full-time dev vs fractional partnership
blocks.intro: Ballpark for a Quebec startup — we refine on a call.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Salary + burden + benefits; often the heaviest line in the company.
rows.fractionalSenior: Comparable band for ~40% of an experienced senior's time.

rows.fullTimeJunior: 2–4 months hiring, onboarding, learning your domain.
rows.fractionalSenior: Start in weeks; architecture and delivery impact from month one.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Depends on level; founder often remains default decision-maker.
rows.fractionalSenior: Explicit ownership of direction, trade-offs, and production quality.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Person to reposition, lay off, or replace.
rows.fractionalSenior: Scope or days adjust without a major social rupture.

rows.fullTimeJunior: 5 days on code — useful when scope is already well defined.
rows.fractionalSenior: 2 focused days + async between sessions; enough for direction and unblocking when framed well.

items.title: We need someone on code every day
items.content:
Often what you need daily is clear decisions and a backlog that moves — not yet five days of unsupervised development. Two days from a senior who structures and unblocks often beats five days from someone still learning your context.

When code volume truly justifies full-time, we'll see it together — and you'll hire with criteria already tested.

items.title: Will we still hire full-time later?
items.content: Yes, if that's the right model. My role is to get you to that moment better prepared: architecture, process, realistic job description. I can support onboarding so continuity doesn't break.

items.title: How does this work with the founder or CEO?
items.content: I partner with you: product priorities, technical reviews, ceremonies when useful. You keep the vision; I bring technical translation and delivery without turning you into a default CTO.

blocks.label: Talk about your first tech hire
blocks.teaser: Post-raise and torn between hiring and going fractional? A 30-minute call is enough to see if two days a week make sense for you.
